title: Expose `--dependency` for `podman run`
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**


/kind feature

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

There's already a field in the container metadata (`podman inspect xxx`) called `Dependencies`, which could perfectly solve the problem of container dependency (e.g. apps should be run after the database). But there's no such way to add dependency when create the container. The only place it's used now is for the pod `pause` container.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman run --dep postgres alpine:latest`


**Describe the results you received:**

No such a parameter

**Describe the results you expected:**

There is such a parameter

comments.body:
@mheon, can you add a check for the `PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT` env var and throw a warning in case it's set?

A Podman-side dependency management can conflict with a systemd-side one (starting sequence, potential dead locks, etc). I think users should chose between the two but not mix.

> I think users should chose between the two but not mix.

That's tricky. The `podman generate systemd` depends on the podman-side dependency. You may drop the flag in the generated systemd units.

I don't think it's tricky (I wrote most of generate systemd). The dependencies are taken into account and are especially important for pods but I don't think we should mix podman-side and systemd-side dependency management.

If users wish to do that, we should allow it but a warning may spare some from a longer debugging journey.

> The dependencies are taken into account and are especially important for pods but I don't think we should mix podman-side and systemd-side dependency management.

Sure. Just want to make sure it doesn't break generate systemd for pods
